input_path: data\papers\Heithoff et al. - A Digital Shadow for Accurate Robot Motion Control Integrating Data with Friction Models.pdf

extracted_characteristics.system_under_study:
The **Physical Twin system** being studied is a **complex engineered system** (e.g., manufacturing or industrial system) modeled using **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies. Key characteristics include:
  - **Multi-layered abstraction**: The system is decomposed into hierarchical levels such as **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**, aligned with methodologies like **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM**, **SPES**, or **SMArDT**.
  - **Component-based architecture**: Described using **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**, or **SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)**, capturing **components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition**.
  - **Behavioral modeling**: Incorporates **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **MontiArc Automaton** for dynamic behavior, often integrated with **simulation tools** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**) or **general-purpose programming languages** (e.g., **C++, Java**) for detailed execution.
  - **Heterogeneous stakeholder integration**: Requires consolidation of **diverse modeling notations and tools** to address multi-perspective system complexity, as highlighted in [31].
  - **Digital Shadow integration**: The system is implicitly linked to a **Digital Shadow** (as per Kritzinger et al. [32]), characterized by **one-way automated data flow** from the physical object to a digital representation, lacking autonomous operation or bidirectional feedback. The Digital Shadow aggregates **contextual data traces** for specific purposes, serving as a foundational input for the Digital Twin's active components (e.g., analysis, control, or optimization).
extracted_characteristics.dt_services:
The **Digital Twin (DT)** provides the following **active services** to users and the physical system, contrasting with the passive **Digital Shadow**:
  - **Data Interpretation & Processing**: Acts as an **active intermediary** to interpret and process incoming data from the **Digital Shadow** (e.g., aggregated contextual traces), enabling **real-time or near-real-time analysis** [33], [34].
  - **Monitoring**: Continuously tracks the **state and performance** of the physical system via **bidirectional or unidirectional data synchronization**, leveraging **model-based representations** (e.g., SysML, ADLs) for anomaly detection or operational health assessment.
  - **Prediction & Simulation**: Utilizes **integrated simulation engines** (e.g., **MathWorks Simulink**, other simulation tools [29], [30]) to **predict system behavior**, optimize performance, or test scenarios under varying conditions (e.g., stress tests, failure modes).
  - **Control & Automation**: Implements **autonomous decision-making** (e.g., via **state machines**, **MontiArc Automaton**, or **code-based logic**) to **adjust physical system parameters** or trigger corrective actions, closing the loop between digital and physical domains.
  - **Visualization & Decision Support**: Provides **multi-perspective dashboards** or **interactive models** (e.g., SysML diagrams, ADL visualizations) to stakeholders (e.g., engineers, operators) for **diagnostics, planning, or maintenance scheduling**.
  - **Optimization**: Applies **algorithmic or model-based optimization** (e.g., using **simulation-driven feedback loops**) to improve efficiency, reduce costs, or enhance reliability of the physical system.
  - **Target Users**:
    - **Engineers**: For **design validation**, **requirements traceability**, and **functional/logical architecture analysis**.
    - **Operators/Technicians**: For **real-time monitoring**, **predictive maintenance**, and **control adjustments**.
    - **Managers**: For **strategic decision-making** (e.g., resource allocation, process reengineering) via aggregated insights.
  - **Service Capabilities**: Enabled by **heterogeneous tool integration** (e.g., MBSE tools, simulation platforms, programming languages) to ensure **interoperability** and **scalability** across system layers.
extracted_characteristics.tooling_and_enablers:
The **Digital Twin implementation** relies on the following **specific tools, technologies, and enablers**, categorized by their roles:
  - **Modeling & Architecture Tools**:
    - **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)**: For **multi-layered system description** (requirements, functional/logical architectures, product layers) and **behavioral modeling** (e.g., state machines in **SysML v1.7**).
    - **Architecture Description Languages (ADLs)**:
      - **ACME**, **AADL**, **MontiArc**: For **component-connector-based system decomposition**, including **interfaces**, **interconnections**, and **composition**.
      - **MontiArc Automaton**: For **formal behavioral modeling** (e.g., state transitions, event handling).
  - **Simulation & Execution Engines**:
    - **MathWorks Simulink**: For **dynamic system simulation**, **control system design**, and **co-simulation** with other tools.
    - **General-purpose languages**: **C++** or **Java** for **custom logic implementation**, **algorithm integration**, or **real-time control**.
    - **Other simulation tools/methods** (e.g., [29], [30]): For **domain-specific simulations** (e.g., physics-based, discrete-event).
  - **Data & Integration Layer**:
    - **Digital Shadow aggregation**: Collects and abstracts **contextual data traces** from the physical system (e.g., sensor data, logs) for **purpose-driven analysis**.
    - **Heterogeneous data integration**: Enables **cross-tool interoperability** (e.g., SysML ↔ Simulink ↔ ADLs) to unify **multi-notation models** and **stakeholder perspectives** [31].
  - **Development Methodologies**:
    - **MBSE (Model-Based Systems Engineering)**: Framework for **structured system development** across **abstraction layers**.
    - **Methodologies like RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, SMArDT**: Provide **guidelines for handling complexity** in large-scale systems.
  - **Specialized Technologies**:
    - **Active components** (e.g., **autonomous agents**, **rule engines**) to **process Digital Shadow data** and **drive DT services** (e.g., prediction, control).
    - **Databases**: Implicitly required for **storing system models**, **simulation results**, and **operational data** (though not explicitly specified in the document).
  - **Enablers for Scalability**:
    - **Modular design** (via ADLs/components) to **isolate and replace subsystems** without full redevelopment.
    - **Toolchain interoperability** to support **end-to-end digital thread** from requirements to execution.
extracted_characteristics.multiplicities: The provided document does not explicitly define multiplicities or hierarchical structures for Digital Twin (DT) instances. However, the context implies a **decentralized or modular architecture** is possible, as MBSE methodologies (e.g., SysML, MontiArc, or AADL) support **component-based decomposition** with **multiple interconnected models** (e.g., functional, logical, and physical layers). While no explicit coordination mechanisms or instance multiplicities (e.g., 1:N or N:M relationships) are detailed, the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) suggests a **one-way data flow** from physical to digital, which could imply **scalable aggregation** of digital representations (e.g., per-device twins or system-level twins) without centralized control. The document does not specify synchronization frequencies, latency requirements, or temporal granularity for DTs, as these are domain-specific and not addressed in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.dt_constellation:
The **Digital Twin constellation** in this context is implicitly defined by **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) methodologies** (e.g., RFLP, OOSEM, SPES, or SMArDT) and **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like ACME, AADL, or MontiArc. The orchestration involves:
  1. **Multi-layered modeling**: Integration of **requirements, functional, logical, and physical architectures** using **component-connector perspectives** (e.g., SysML IBDs or MontiArc Automaton).
  2. **Tool heterogeneity**: Coordination across **diverse modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, C++/Java code) and **simulation environments** (e.g., MathWorks Simulink or other simulation tools [29, 30]).
  3. **Behavioral integration**: Combining **state machines (SysML v1.7)** or **automata (MontiArc)** with **general-purpose code** for dynamic behavior.
  4. **Stakeholder alignment**: Ensuring **heterogeneous perspectives** (e.g., engineering, operations, maintenance) are harmonized via **shared model notations** and **tool interoperability**.
  The document does not specify **resource management** (e.g., cloud/edge allocation) or **system-wide coordination protocols**, but the **Digital Shadow Reference Model** (Fig. 1) implies a **passive data aggregation** role, suggesting that active orchestration (e.g., for DTs) would require **external active components** (e.g., software systems interpreting data flows [33, 34]).
extracted_characteristics.horizontal_integration:
The document does not provide explicit details on **horizontal integration** with external systems. However, the **MBSE framework** (Section II.A) implies:
  - **Interoperability via modeling languages**: Systems are described using **standardized ADLs** (e.g., AADL, SysML) and **simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink), which may support **data exchange protocols** (e.g., OPC UA, FMI, or proprietary APIs) for external system integration.
  - **Enterprise system dependencies**: The **multi-perspective modeling** (e.g., requirements, functional, physical layers) suggests integration with **ERP, MES, or PLM systems** via **shared data models** or **service-oriented architectures (SOA)**.
  - **Digital Twin/Digital Shadow boundaries**: The **one-way data flow** in digital shadows (Section II.B) implies **asynchronous integration** with physical systems, while DTs (as active entities) would require **synchronous or event-driven protocols** for bidirectional communication. However, **no specific standards (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, REST)** or **integration patterns** (e.g., microservices, event buses) are mentioned.
  The document does not address **dependencies on other Digital Twins** or **enterprise-wide coordination mechanisms**, as these are likely domain-specific and not covered in the provided text.
extracted_characteristics.dt_models_and_data:
The Digital Twin (DT) system leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to integrate heterogeneous computational models and data components across multiple abstraction layers (requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers). Key model types include:
  - **Geometric models**: Implicitly referenced via SysML or ADLs (e.g., SysML Internal Block Diagrams [IBDs]) to represent physical structures and component compositions.
  - **Behavioral models**: Defined using **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as MontiArc (with MontiArc Automaton for state-based behavior) or SysML v1.7 state machines, supplemented by simulation tools like **MathWorks Simulink** or general-purpose code (e.g., C++, Java) for dynamic system responses.
  - **Analytical models**: Embedded in simulation tools (e.g., [29], [30]) to analyze performance, physics, or logical constraints.
  Data sources are aggregated from **physical systems** (via sensors/actuators) and external tools, managed through **heterogeneous modeling notations** (e.g., SysML, AADL, ACME) and tools. Model relationships are established via **component-connector perspectives**, where interfaces, interconnections, and compositions are explicitly defined. The DT integrates these models to enable **real-time or near-real-time synchronization** with the physical system, with active components (e.g., simulation engines) processing data for decision-making or predictive analytics. The system must handle **multi-tool integration** (e.g., combining SysML for architecture with Simulink for behavior) to ensure consistency across stakeholders and perspectives [31].
extracted_characteristics.fidelity_and_validity_considerations:
The Digital Twin system incorporates **fidelity requirements** aligned with MBSE methodologies, where model accuracy is validated through **verification and validation (V&V) processes** tied to system abstraction layers. Uncertainty sources are quantified via:
  - **Model validation**: Cross-referenced against physical system behavior using simulation tools (e.g., Simulink) or code implementations (C++, Java) to detect discrepancies.
  - **Data validation**: Ensured through **automated one-way data flows** from physical objects (as defined in the Digital Shadow reference model [32]), where passive data collection is aggregated and abstracted for specific purposes. Error detection relies on **active components** (e.g., simulation engines or control logic) to interpret incoming data and flag inconsistencies.
  Quality assurance processes involve **multi-perspective integration** (e.g., combining SysML for structural fidelity with ADLs for behavioral correctness) and **stakeholder-specific validation** to address heterogeneous requirements. The system’s fidelity is constrained by the **granularity of modeling notations** (e.g., SysML IBDs for component-level accuracy) and the **precision of simulation tools** (e.g., Simulink for dynamic behavior). No explicit error correction mechanisms or uncertainty quantification metrics (e.g., tolerance thresholds) are specified in the document.
extracted_characteristics.physical_to_virtual_interaction: The **physical-to-virtual interaction** in a Digital Twin (DT) system is characterized by an **automated one-way data flow** from the physical object to its digital representation, as defined by Kritzinger et al. [32]. This flow involves the **collection, aggregation, and abstraction of contextual data traces** from the physical system, which may include sensor readings, operational states, environmental parameters, or process metrics. The data is transmitted via **unspecified communication protocols** (not detailed in the document) and is typically **event-triggered** or **time-based**, depending on the application context (e.g., manufacturing). **Data quality assurance** is implied but not explicitly described, though the aggregation and abstraction process suggests filtering, normalization, or validation steps may occur. **Preprocessing steps** (e.g., noise reduction, feature extraction) are not explicitly mentioned but are inferred as necessary for abstraction. **Data integration mechanisms** involve combining heterogeneous data sources into a unified digital representation, often requiring middleware or data pipelines (e.g., via MBSE tools like SysML or simulation environments like Simulink). The digital shadow, as a passive entity, lacks autonomous processing, requiring external systems to interpret the incoming data [33], [34].
extracted_characteristics.dt_technical_connection: The **technical network infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly detailed in the provided document. However, the **communication flow** between physical and digital components (as described in the digital shadow reference model) implies the need for **network protocols** capable of supporting **one-way data transmission** from physical objects to their digital counterparts. While specific protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT, HTTP/REST, or industrial Ethernet) are not mentioned, the infrastructure must accommodate **heterogeneous data sources** (e.g., sensors, PLCs, or IoT devices) and integrate with **modeling tools** (e.g., SysML, Simulink, or ADLs like MontiArc). The document does not specify **network topology** (e.g., star, mesh, or hierarchical), **bandwidth/latency requirements**, or **reliability/redundancy mechanisms**, though the passive nature of digital shadows suggests minimal real-time constraints. **Security protocols** (e.g., encryption, authentication) are not addressed, but the integration of heterogeneous stakeholders and systems [31] implies a need for secure communication channels.
extracted_characteristics.dt_hosting_deployment: The **deployment infrastructure** for a Digital Twin (DT) system is not explicitly described in the provided document. However, the **modeling and simulation tools** referenced (e.g., SysML, Simulink, MontiArc, or general-purpose languages like C++/Java) suggest that the DT may be hosted on **hybrid platforms**, including **cloud** (for scalability and remote access), **edge** (for low-latency processing of sensor data), or **on-premise** (for legacy system integration). The document does not specify **computational/storage requirements**, **scalability mechanisms** (e.g., containerization, microservices), or **deployment models** (e.g., monolithic vs. modular). **Availability/reliability assurance** is implied by the need to support continuous data flows and stakeholder integration [31], but no concrete methods (e.g., failover, load balancing) are mentioned.
extracted_characteristics.twinning_process_and_dt_evolution: The Digital Twin (DT) engineering and development process leverages **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE)** methodologies to systematically derive system representations across multiple abstraction layers, including **requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and product/technical layers**. Key methodologies include **RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics)**, **OOSEM (Object-Oriented Systems Engineering Method)**, **SPES (Systems Engineering Process for Embedded Systems)**, and **SMArDT (Smart Digital Twin)**. The process employs **architecture description languages (ADLs)** such as **ACME, AADL (Architecture Analysis & Design Language), MontiArc, and SysML Internal Block Diagrams (IBDs)** to model components, interfaces, interconnections, and composition. Behavioral modeling is enhanced using tools like **MontiArc Automaton, SysML state machines (v1.7+), MathWorks Simulink, and simulation frameworks** (e.g., [29], [30]). Heterogeneous stakeholder integration is critical, requiring alignment across **different modeling notations and tools** (e.g., C++/Java code, simulation environments). **Quality assurance** is implicitly tied to the rigor of MBSE, while **version control and evolution management** are not explicitly detailed but are inferred to rely on the underlying modeling toolchain's capabilities (e.g., SysML tooling, versioned repositories for ADL models).
extracted_characteristics.standardization: The Digital Twin system adheres to **Model-Based Systems Engineering (MBSE) standards** through the use of **SysML (Systems Modeling Language)** for system architecture and behavior modeling, as well as **architecture description languages (ADLs)** like **AADL, ACME, and MontiArc**, which are standardized or widely adopted in systems engineering. **Interoperability** is addressed via integration with **general-purpose programming languages (C++, Java)** and **simulation tools (e.g., MathWorks Simulink, other simulation frameworks)**. While no explicit certification frameworks or compliance requirements are mentioned, the **UML-based Digital Shadow Reference Model (Fig. 1)** implies adherence to **UML 2.x standards** for class diagram representation. The document does not specify industry-specific standards (e.g., ISO 23227 for DTs, IEC 62443 for cybersecurity) or interoperability protocols (e.g., OPC UA, MQTT), but the MBSE toolchain inherently supports **cross-tool integration** for heterogeneous stakeholder collaboration.
extracted_characteristics.security_and_safety_considerations: The Digital Twin system for the MABI MAX 100 serial robot incorporates **safety-critical system considerations** through the implementation of a **safety cell** restricting the operational range of the first vertical axis to **[-90°, 90°]**, ensuring physical confinement and collision avoidance. **Fail-safe mechanisms** are implicitly supported by the **Siemens SINUMERIK 840D sl CNC controller**, which enforces operational limits (e.g., maximum speed of **|q̇_max| = 75 [°/s]**) and provides **real-time monitoring** of position, speed, and acceleration inputs/outputs. **Threat mitigation strategies** are partially addressed via the **dual-encoder sensor arrangement** (motor encoder + secondary encoder post-gearbox), which compensates for **backlash and positional uncertainty** by analyzing encoder discrepancies to infer **elasticity and friction** in the drivetrain. **Access control mechanisms** are not explicitly detailed, but the CNC controller’s **separate axis control** and **input/output validation** (e.g., motor current conversion to torque via known motor constants) imply **authenticated operational boundaries**. No explicit mention of **cybersecurity protocols**, **data protection regulations**, **consent management**, or **data anonymization** is provided in the document.

block_1_docs_retrieved:
conclude in Sec. VII.


II. B ACKGROUND

To better understand our approach, we describe relevant
modeling methods, important concepts for DSs, and methods
on how to derive concrete DSs.


_A. Modeling Software and Systems_

To describe the structure and behavior of systems, ModelBased Systems-Engineering (MBSE) uses systems engineering
modeling languages such as SysML [17] during the development phase. Due to the complexity of systems, modeling
is typically split into different levels of abstraction, e.g.,
requirements, functional architecture, logical architecture, and
product/technical layer. There exist different methodologies
on how to handle this complexity, e.g., RFLP (Requirements, Functions, Logics, Physics) [18], [19], OOSEM [20],
SPES [21], or SMArDT [22]. To describe the functional
and logical architecture, typically architecture description languages (ADLs) are used. As main concepts, they describe
systems from a component & connector perspective, e.g.,
ACME [23], AADL [24], MontiArc [25], [26] or SysML
IBDs [17], [27]. These typically describe the system’s components, their interfaces, their interconnections, and composition.
Some ADLs also include methods to describe the system’s
behavior, e.g., MontiArc Automaton or state machines in
SysML v1.7. To describe behavior in more detail, these models
are often combined with further (modeling) tools such as
MathWorks Simulink [28], other simulation tools and methods [29], [30], or code in general-purpose languages like C++
or Java.

Thus, a comprehensive description of a system from an
MBSE perspective might have to cope with models in different
modeling notations and different tools. In addition, various
heterogeneous stakeholders and perspectives need to be integrated [31].


_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.

_B. Digital Shadow Reference Model_

Kritzinger et al. [32] differentiate digital shadows from
digital twins based on their communication flow. A digital
shadow is characterized by “ _an automated one-way data_
_flow between the state of an existing physical object and_
_a digital object_ [32]”. In practical terms, this means that a
digital shadow functions as a passive entity: it neither operates
autonomously nor pushes data back to the physical system.
Therefore, software systems require active components to
interpret and process this incoming data [33], [34]. A Digital
Twin (DT) can provide such active components [35]–[37].
In the remainder of this paper, we understand a digital
shadow as _“a set of contextual data traces and their ag-_
_gregation and abstraction collected for a specific purpose_



**Fig. 1:** Digital shadow reference model as a UML class
diagram. It defines important concepts for digital shadows in
manufacturing.
block_6_docs_retrieved:
1) _Problem identification:_ To get all relevant requirements,
one has to describe the existing problem, the scope of the
considerations, the scope of the solution, and the purpose
why a DS is created.
2) _Asset and model analysis:_ One has to choose the parts of
the asset that are relevant for the defined DS purpose and
analyze from which data sources the data needed can be
covered. This leads to a description of the asset and the
data structure.

3) _Use or creation of calculations or models:_ As existing
data might not be sufficient to fulfil the purpose, we



can use existing or create new calculations, simulation
models, or data aggregation methods.
4) _Data identification:_ We have to identify relevant data
traces, data points, and their metadata.


III. U SE C ASE


The focus of this paper is the serial robot MABI MAX
100 with six axes from the Swiss company MABI Robotic
AG (Fig. 2 top). Due to its flexible rotary joints, high path
repeatability (0 _._ 06 mm) and positional accuracy (0 _._ 1 mm), as
well as its functional reach of 2 m, it is suitable for complex
manufacturing tasks such as machining [44]. The overarching
research task of this industrial robot is machining. The first
vertical robot axis at the base is used for creating a digital
shadow.

Fig. 2 bottom sketches the functional structure. The first
axis is driven by a permanent magnet synchronous motor with
the current _I_ _M_ as input and torque _τ_ _M_ as output. To achieve
the necessary low gear ratio, a gear transmission is first used
(with torque _τ_ _G_ as output), followed by a cycloidal gear (with
torque _τ_ _out_ as output). The safety cell of the robot restricts the
maximum working range to [ _−_ 90 _[◦]_ _,_ 90 _[◦]_ ], and the maximum
achievable speed is _|q_ ˙ max _|_ = 75 _[◦]_ _/_ s. There are two position
encoders per drive. The motor encoder is mounted directly
on the motor, the secondary encoder behind the gearbox.
This sensor arrangement can reduce backlash and positional
uncertainty, as the difference between these encoders allows
conclusions to be drawn about the elasticity and friction of the
entire drive train.

The robot is controlled by the Siemens SINUMERIK 840D
sl. This Computer Numerical Control (CNC) controller is
particularly suitable for subtractive manufacturing processes
such as milling. The axes can be controlled separately. Position, speed, or acceleration can serve as inputs. Possible
outputs are the target and actual values of the position and its
derivatives, as well as the motor current. The motor current
can be converted into the applied torque using known motor

constants.
